Genetic Diet

Genetic Diet Adaptations

To Agriculture

Going from hunting to an agricultural life changed how humans and dogs fundamentally lived. Dogs used to help humans capture prey, but now the Neolithic dogs changed from their carnivorous diet, and began consuming plant-based starches. This change in diet was possible through new biological changes in genes.

Genetic Adaptation: Starch Digestion

Wild wolves carried two copies of the AMY2B gene which helps digesting carbs. The newly domesticated dogs living in the new villages began mutating to have more of those genes, as well as other carb digesting genes to help them slowly function in this new era of agriculture.

Scholarly Evidence:

"We narrow the plausible range for the date of initial dog domestication to an interval spanning 11–16 thousand years ago, predating the rise of agriculture. In light of this finding, we expand upon previous work regarding the increase in copy number of the amylase gene (AMY2B) in dogs, which is believed to have aided digestion of starch in agricultural refuse."

(Freedman Abstract)

"Dog domestication as accompanied by selection at three genes with key roles in starch digestion: AMY2B, MGAM and SGLT1. Our results show that adaptations that allowed the early ancestors of modern dogs to thrive on a diet rich in starch… constituted a crucial step in early dog domestication… development of agriculture catalysed the domestication of dogs"

(Axelsson 363)

"Selection for increased AMY2B copy number did not take place during the early stage of domestication… and did not become widespread until several thousand years after the first appearance of starch-rich agricultural lifestyles"

(Bergström 557–564)

My Research Findings:

The data and figure shows definite trend relating to agriculture. The change from hunting to farming did not just change human civilization permanently, but it changed biological markers in our first domesticated animal. This change altered canines to adapt to the crops we grew.
